author: @pjhalchemy
text: Robert, This video got my sub...well done and said!!  I've been watching for a few months, enjoying your enthusiasm and bringing ideas forward with some basic testing.  I am also very passionate about the same things you are and particularly wind as a source of power generation for about 5-6 decades now.  It's So refreshing to hear someone talk about discernment and research in the modern world of pet rock phenomena.  White papers are where I usually start if something catches my attention.  Thanks for the Google Scholar tip...haven't tried that yet.  I even downloaded your STL files on this as it is "Appealing in concept" thinking I'd like to test this design...rigorously with protocols.  Back in the day I designed and built a 70' 36kcfm 100HP wind tunnel to AMCA specs and certified by them and may still have access to it for testing.  But as you said it's important to test live generation as well.  Would like to hear your thoughts on what type of generator and how to build it in or external drive it?  Also curious how scalable this design is.  Thanks for lighting my candle once again!!  Hat Tip, ~PJ

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: He is part of something shady and will never admit this, but unless you model the turbine using CAD, optimize it using CFD, 3D print or assemble it and benchmark its output power, any conclusion is useless (he never does it for example). There is a video here showing three similar looking turbines with similar prices bought from a supermarket and only one of them had the expected output power, the other two were useless.

author: @jamesclark5093
text:
Can anyone explain what this is saying in layman's terms ? Thanks
Toroidal tank evaluationThe reported study had been conducted to determine the feasibility of developing toroidal tanks for an auxiliary propulsion system that could be integrated into a Pioneer spin-stabilized spacecraft. The system evaluated consisted of two toroidal tanks equipped with surface tension devices. One tank is intended for nitrogen tetroxide, the other for monomethylhydrazine. The study included the definition of a propellant acquisition system concept that could satisfy the requirements of a spinning tank. It was found that an oxidizer tank spin rate of 76 rpm and a fuel tank spin rate of 110 rpm would be required to produce liquid loss from the acquisition system during spacecraft operation. An investigation showed that toroidal tanks can be fabricated with present technology using either titanium or aluminum alloys. The preferred material would be titanium because of its significantly higher strength.

replies.author: @SoundzAlive1
replies.text: The passage describes a study on using toroidal tanks (donut-shaped fuel containers) in a spacecraft for an extra propulsion system. Two tanks, one for nitrogen tetroxide and the other for monomethylhydrazine, were considered. The study explored a system to extract fuel from these spinning tanks during spacecraft operation. It found that specific spin rates were needed for optimal functioning. The tanks can be made with titanium or aluminum alloys, but titanium is preferred due to its greater strength.André

author: @jamesross1003
text: The O-wind does work,  how well is debatable.  A beach ball held in place by cups at top and bottom with those cups attached to generators is slightly less effective.  So take everything with a grain of salt.  Thanks Rob!

text: oWind - iPhone. Steve Jobs's genius was taking an easily duplicable product and creating a cult around it. They have a proven history of success that can't be reduced to clever marketing. Keep going Robert!

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: The IPhone was based on well known and solid operating system for desktop computers (macOS) but scaled down and the technology was superior to the rest (at the time), it wasn't easy to duplicate at all. Even nowadays the Android OS is still inferior to iOS.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @mikeyb7263Every previous attempt to build a smartphone was completely stupid for obvious reasons (Java VMs, garbage OSes, unproven UI technologies, ...) and used inferior hardware. So stupid it isn't close to compete with the iPhone's base software even after decades.

author: @ajempower
text: I am a fan and note that you like wind and fire, me too. One thing that escapes me is that as an island surrounded by water whose mass volume movements are predictable on a daily basis. Storms being the occasional exception of the volume and power and not of timing. Why are we not investing in the most predictable source of green energy? Tides come in and go out every day pulled by the proximity of the Moon.

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: the real issue there is access, not many people own a bit of land with access to the sea (and thus the tides) and a lot of the rest is owned publicly (where installation would be frowned upon) so individual access to a tidal solution is not as easy as wind where you can stick up a pole with an experimental turbine from any piece of land you own. for larger corporations and companies- yes tidal power is a possibility, but for me its diversity that rules, not just one means to get power, but lots of different ones, currently the biggest hold up to generating more energy is the distribution network, so we need to generate at point of use so we arent dependent upon the distribution network to get the energy to where it is needed.

author: @larrygodbold3017
text: Thanks for your usual humorous, insightful video. Back in the '70s when I was first becoming interested in wind generators, one of the few books I ever found with good information stated that the most efficient design is thin blades on a horizontal axis ( HAWT ) . One is best ( counterbalanced ) , two is next best ( but they break when changing direction in variable winds ) so the best compromise is three thin blades on a horizontal axis ( HAWT ). Three thin blades on a vertical  axis ( VAWT ) are good but not self-starting. The good HAWTs can reach 20%. The rest of that stuff is hype.

replies.author: @thekaxmax
replies.text: so the o-wind is an attempt to re-present the VAWT, then.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
VAWT can be self starting but needs either variable incidence blades, or a drag type accessory - sprag coupled or a speed controlled decoupling device reduces high speed drag..

In Aerodynamics it is tricky to abandon aspect ratio.  If the aspect ratio disappears from the design language - so too does the power.

replies.author: @wiradharma7030
replies.text:
I think there is a need for a charge pump sections to harvest ripple of generated energy. A switching mechanism might ne needed for switching between low and high energy mode. A sense resistor controlled by microcontroller might be needed to control mosfets(low rds one) to switch between low and high circuit.

Charge pump consist of coils, capacitors and switching ic. Most of the available circuit use transistor, but a small mosfet might be better as transistor have 0.7v dropping voltage. Using transistor 0.7v are wasted, so it could only works on 0.7v and above.
